name: LMCF (Low Movecount Corners First)
description: LMCF (Low Movecount Corners First) is a corners-first method optimized for minimal move count. It solves all eight corners first, then places the E-layer edges (E2L), and finishes with the last five or six edges (L5E/L6E). Unlike the classic Waterman corners-first approach, LMCF uses modern techniques to achieve an impressively low move count while remaining relatively accessible. It is popular among cubers interested in efficient solving without heavy algorithm memorization.

steps.name: Solve All Corners
steps.description: Solve all eight corners of the cube. This typically involves solving the first layer corners intuitively, then using CLL or Ortega-style techniques to orient and permute the last layer corners.
tips: First layer corners can be solved intuitively
CLL handles the last layer corners in one step
Solving corners first gives a unique cube perspective

steps.name: E2L (Equator Two Layers / E-slice Edges)
steps.description: Place the four equatorial (E-slice / middle layer) edges into their correct positions. With all corners solved, these edges can be inserted using M and U moves without disturbing the corners.
tips: Use M and U moves to cycle edges into position
Track all four E-slice edges before starting

steps.name: L5E / L6E (Last 5 or 6 Edges)
steps.description: Solve the remaining five or six edges (depending on whether one edge was solved during E2L). This step uses M and U moves to orient and permute the remaining edges, completing the solve.
tips: Edge orientation should be tracked from earlier steps
M U generators can solve most cases
Break into sub-steps: orient edges, then permute
L5E is similar to LSE in Roux but with different constraints

pros: Ergonomic M and U moves in later steps
cons: Corners-first thinking is unfamiliar to most solvers
L5E/L6E can be slow without practice
Limited resources compared to mainstream methods
Edge steps require good M-slice fluency
